most people think the only water resistant option there is for clothing is plastic/polyester fabrics

cotton impregnated with beeswax/ linseed oil (🚫 paraffine)  and wool impregnated with lanolin are way better, more durable options that you can keep re-proofing

having lanolin, beeswax and linseed oil at home is always a good idea. there is always a garment in need of water/tear proofing, a chopping board in need of conditioning, or a dry knee in need of moisturizing.

1- the claim is that putting infants to sleep on their backs in a firm surface causes plagiocephaly (deformity of the cranium) but have you ever seen a newborn on a flat surface? their heads don’t stay still!
2- however if you observe a baby in a baby container like a car seat, bouncer, etc. where their head is cradled around all sides, you’ll notice they can’t turn their heads properly
3- how are we to believe sleeping on their backs is causing babies to have plagiocephaly instead of the baby containers that restrict head movement??
4- a literature review from 2020 states that most studies on this are observational and with high selection and performance bias (Hewitt et al 2020)
5- also they found an indeterminate association between tummy time and plagiocephaly (whodda thunk??)

6- i wonder if the positives correlated  with tummy time are due to other factors (for example the higher BMI in non tummy time babies to me seems more likely to be from social factors - lower income families have higher formula feeding rates and higher BMI babies)

i just read simon sarris’ post on agency in childhood/education and now i’m thinking of a hilarious booklet i have about the work of a russian psychologist called pavel blonsky who idealized this kind of crazy socialist education system
i’ll have to post some quotes and translate them because it’s so precious
@Coscorrodrift siii

basically he idealized that between kindergarten and school children would go on these trips to the countryside to live like primitive humans and experience the progress of technology and civilization first-hand https://t.co/XtXNsUuEVj
some quotes: “They realize that the hand can produce numerous things and that the stick and the stone are the most ancient ancestors of modern tools”

“The ‘robinsonade’ opens children’s eyes to life, labour and the genius of man. The child feels the difference between the stone age and the iron age, and [] feels very much naturally attracted to the iron tools, that find their highest level of perfection in the machine.”

a popular ‘respectful parenting’ portuguese influencer has made her second post on why she thinks natural consequences aren’t respectful to children and i’m 🙄
her argument: if my daughter’s play dough was left uncovered or she forgot to water her plant, I would simply do those things for her and tell her about it, because that’s more respectful and loving
she goes on to say she thinks that natural consequences (in this case, letting the play dough dry out or the plant die) are punishments in disguise

my sister in christ, they are the punishments of life itself!
of course I would intervene in case of something super important emotionally to a child or if it concerned for example an animal’s wellbeing, but there’s a reason we advocate for natural consequences in montessori

young children (unlike adults) don’t yet know that play dough will dry and plants will die, and the only way they will truly understand that is by witnessing it and connecting the (in)action to its natural, logical consequence

life will present these learning opportunities for them, whether you want that to happen or not.
having something like this happen when you have a parent or educator there to comfort you, explain it to you and give you tools to deal with it *is* the best case scenario

these same ppl usually say that children shouldnt see parents get upset/cry over something that the child did because they will develop guilt. my dudes 😫 it’s natural to get sad and react to things that upset us! children need to see that happen, too. they need to see us cope.

also it seems like the notion that you *should* treat children differently than you would an adult friend is becoming unpopular in these circles… a rejection of the authority and responsibility that comes w being a parent/educator

I feel like acting this way deprives kids of seeing the world as it is. It keeps them in this bubble where stuff gets put away, fixed and watered magically.
Our job is not to shield them from the world completely, but to give them tools and support to deal with the hard parts.

I was recently told by a well respected Montessori trainer “never intervene if intention is positive, always intervene if intention is negative, always intervene before chaos erupts” but

@_samantha_joy @elproximus @mbateman I don’t really think I can/should(?) project my idea of positive or negative intention onto a toddler’s action so I tend to lean more into the RIE approach of only preventing harm and allowing the interaction to play out. i’ve been surprised with how frequently it solves itself
@_samantha_joy @elproximus @mbateman both of these happen. also they get distracted by other things or people, or they find a way to play together/alongside each other

@_samantha_joy @elproximus @mbateman good points! i think if you observe a child who is regularly the victim or the aggressor in many scenarios you have something to inspect and deal with
about focus - if a child seems very concentrated it’s probably more important to hold the boundary of their work and space 1/2

@_samantha_joy @elproximus @mbateman in order to preserve focus and because in that moment where they are absorbed in work they are less likely to be alert to their surroundings and able to stand up for themselves 2/2

@MonaStoicescu @_samantha_joy @elproximus @mbateman imo it’s a gentle balancing act between “defending” a child’s right to their space and things and allowing them the opportunity to figure out how to manage those conflicts themselves in an emotionally supportive environment

@MonaStoicescu @_samantha_joy @elproximus @mbateman in a conflict between babies and toddlers (and even older children) there often isn’t a clear distinction on who did what to who, and both parties might get upset for fair reasons… in that case it’s really hard for an adult to step in and play judge without doing it unfairly

🧵documenting operation floor sleeping

i've decided to start sleeping on the floor full time, to help improve my back pain and mobility in general

if you want to learn more abt why one would do that, listen to this https://t.co/paz5ovDpjN
however i live in a small space and i can't gradually start sleeping on the floor while my bed is still here as it takes up all of the space, so I've decided to just get rid of it

☑️ first step got done today: go through everything in my under-bed storage (mostly out of season clothes and books that don't fit on my shelf), declutter as much as possible and store whatever is left in the wardrobe

next step is taking apart the bed frame and getting rid of it. it's a hand me down from a friend, quite old and beaten up, so i've decided to just leave it on the road for someone else to take (maybe listing it on marketplace for free with the location)
I'll leave my current mattress on the floor for a bit (no problem rn as the weather is quite hot and dry) while I either sell it or find a new home for it among friends/family

after I part with it, for a while I'll either use a camping mattress or a thin ikea one I have in storage
then I'll get a sheepskin/thin futon as a more permanent solution. this kind of depends on whether I move countries or not, so I'll hold out on that until i'm sure

@odeducionista there are a few cheaper options available. you can get a thin foam mat (i'd go with latex over synthetics if possible) and just be careful to hang it or flip it on its side frequently if you live in a humid climate

@_samantha_joy i HIGHLY recommend solid starts on this! check out their instagram or website. they focus a lot on strategies to prevent picky eating!
@_samantha_joy The shelf help podcast has great episodes on this topic, namely S1E45, S2E5, S2E23

@_samantha_joy one tool they suggest is the division of responsibility:
- caretaker is responsible for what is offered, when and where
- child is responsible for what they eat from the options and how much

to further support choice you can involve children in making a meal plan, for example

@_samantha_joy they suggest that instead of “finish your meal” or “you have to try N bites” you should hold firm with “you don’t have to eat it if you don’t want to, but that is what’s on the menu today”
it’s very montessorian imo: you use the environment to expose and encourage children to try
@_samantha_joy new things, but the caretaker then takes a step back and allows the child to make their choice

@seanjs @_samantha_joy The difference here is that in Montessori we purposefully choose and present materials that would best stimulate the child’s development at a particular time, while in RIE choosing something with the purpose of mental stimulation is sort of frowned upon (1/2)

@seanjs @_samantha_joy which, as samantha pointed out, is not very consistent - the adult ends up choosing play objects and what goes in the home and where they lay a baby anyway… it’s simply impossible to avoid curating the child’s experience in the world (2/2)

@seanjs @_samantha_joy Mtss materials are close ended - the self correction is built into the material so that the child works with it in a specific way. in both Mtss and RIE, the child learns thru doing things themselves - but Mtss tasks the adult with this indirect teaching of curating opportunities
unclogging a drain is the most humiliating task for some reason

@seanjs hi - i think i expressed myself poorly. i didn't mean RIE is against mental stimulation, but against the adult choosing a specific material for the child with stimulation in mind (usually in montessori we will choose a material that works on say, object permanence - on purpose)

@seanjs when we think the child would benefit from that type of work. whereas in RIE (as i understand it) you would provide open ended materials all of the time, and the child would find whatever work they needed in the moment in that open ended material.

i greatly underestimated the importance of having other montessori teachers. there is no one else interested in planning lessons or discussing budding skills or creating/adapting materials… all the work falls on me and most days i simply don’t have the time

it’s like they’re only dealing with the day-to-day stuff (requesting diapers, fixing things, etc.) and completely missing that we’re faced with growing people who are changing every day?? i mean i know it’s a lot of work but it should be the priority lmao

it was exciting at first to have full autonomy on things but now it just feels daunting because nobody is keeping my systems alive or improving on them. they don’t even understand the important purposes it serves to have complete, beautiful materials every morning… :(

also the general feeling whenever the children misuse things (pretend play with work mats, drink from the window cleaning spray bottle , etc.) is that they’re not ready, they’re too young, and take those things away
when a few days of consistent redirection prove otherwise

the head of school/ my co-teacher (who did montessori 3-6 assistant training..) thinks 3.5 yr olds are too young and immature to be able to do a full cycle of activity including unrolling and rolling up a mat… so she doesn’t even model it or encourage it
that was my little rant but it’s a challenge i’m optimistic about, and I hope I can change her mind 🤞🤞

this is the order i'm kinda following for the newborn parent-baby classes:
1- observation (already done, impt because they'll be practicing it alongside the classes)
2- absorbent mind + sensitive periods (sprinkling in some more theory, to establish common ground)

3- motor development
4- prepared environment (theoretical), 0-6 home environment (practical)
5- baby commercial equipment, clothing and shoes
6- feeding
7- language development
8 - independence
* 0-6 months

Toddler needs to take lifesaving medication asap, but runs away and refuses

Toddler starts to hit/bite another child at the playground

Baby has a dirty diaper which is causing a painful rash, screams when parent tries to change them

@____SeaBass @TCSparents Usually for me to even consider doing something against a child’s will it either has to be a matter of their own safety, someone else’s safety, a very time-sensitive issue, something massively important/unavoidable that the child can’t understand yet, or most often a combination

@mbateman theories in order of wackiness
-ppl become more cautious as they age and extend that to their children
-older ppl have less kids so they over-infantilize them as a way to extend childhood
-ppl that have prev. put off reproducing for "existential" reasons feel guilty > overprotect

@amelapay from reading a thick anthropological textbook it seems like those babies might not be crying as much bc of learned helplessness (crying not producing a response from caregiver) rather than being in a constant state of content, but our "noble savage" glasses won't let us see that

The more time I spend learning about and caring for small children the more chill I become about it… I don’t sweat the small stuff. I don’t worry about entertaining them or planning special activities. I just do my best with the controllables and let go of everything else.

I do prepare the environment and care about all the details, but I just have a fundamental trust that most things are probably fine and that children will find a way. Even in the worst scenarios kids flourish. Most caregivers that are trying to do a good job are already doing so.

Embracing this trust and chillness I notice makes me a lot less neurotic/ fundamentalist than many of my colleagues. I won’t stress a parent out for giving their baby a pacifier or letting them watch TV even if I don’t think it’s ideal. It’s OK in the grand scheme of things!

Babies/kids can benefit from a change in the environment bc the right, precise nudge, stimuli or removal of obstacles may help them blossom even more AND if we don’t do those things the probability of it being fine is huge. it’s more about lifting the ceiling than the floor

I’m a little bit obsessed with seeing things at different scales at once. being able to zoom in and out of a problem… the details simultaneously matter a lot (take them seriously! fine tune them! 🔎 ) and not at all (relax if they don’t go as you planned 😌)

@frideswyth hm, portuguese toddlers definitely don't sound like they have brazilian accents. I think the cadence is an influence from african bantu languages, which are syllable-timed like brazilian portuguese, and also add vowels to break up consonant pairs CC -> CVC
@frideswyth for example - in portugal the word "pneu" (tire) is pronounced as pnew, whereas in brazil people can say pinew, breaking up the cluster to preserve syllable timing, as in bantu languages.

@frideswyth "você" is used in 2nd person singular in brazil and portuguese-speaking african countries, but is considered incorrect use in european portuguese. to my knowledge it came from old portuguese (from "vossa mercê")

@frideswyth nowadays in EP, to address someone formally in a way that's considered correct, you should use a form of the third person singular where you sub article+noun for the pronoun, for example: "O senhor vai entrar?"

@frideswyth there are other examples of BP keeping words or expressions from old portuguese that didn't stay in use in EP. one that comes to mind is the use of "menor" (smaller) in BP, while in EP it's now considered an "erudite" form, and we mostly say "mais pequeno" (more small) - lol
@frideswyth cool video on several influences on brazilian portuguese, including Tupi and dialects from different parts of portugal (auto translated captions working well) https://t.co/qa2GYQ00Os

it's 1am and obviously i am commenting an essay-long reflection on a video about tummy time
yes i am obsessed with this question but it's because i think it perfectly encapsulates western society's view of a healthy movement life as overall sedentarism + short bursts of intense exercise

and just like with exercise, i don't think it's bad or we should avoid it. but i don't think it should be the main focus or component of a movement-rich lifestyle. sure, some exercise is better than full sedentarism, but it is not a replacement for a balanced movement diet

not sure who needs to read this but forced repetition and practice leads to boredom, resistance and detachment, not deep flow-state concentration, wonder and joyous learning. mind-numbing paperwork for children is still mind-numbing paperwork 👍

Maria Montessori was mega crunchy... Just reading a passage where she declares herself to be anti hospital births because they disregard psychic (ie psychological) health in their birth and postpartum practices
she mentions stuff like how doctors and nurses handle babies, the noise and light in the room, and the babies being separated from the mom when in nature mothers keep their young close

@p_millerd Yeah, at the time she said this (1946) she mentioned that statistics in America showed the percentage of deaths in maternity hospitals to be higher than in homes... and also talked abt rampant infection in hospitals
"Man must be superior to animals: everything that is in nature, he must raise to a greater height. He must not forget the teachings of nature. He must be super natural, not extra natural."

"The hospital is for the sick and this child who is to be born is treated like an illness. Birth is natural, but the child goes where sickness is treated. (...) One woman has a tumor removed, the other brings a baby into the world. (...) This lacks respect for humanity."
I'm obsessed with her concerns over the aesthetic implications of our practices. We still don't pay enough attention to this

I'm pretty sure she would've loved birth centers and the like. She was into having specialized newborn nurses who understood and provided for the baby's psychic needs as well as his physical ones (one of the ideas at the genesis of my training - assistants to infancy!)

"The newborn child should be allowed to remain in fetal position after birth, yet he is immediately dressed and, indeed, at one time he was swaddled, his fragile limbs restrained by force."
if we get timetravel I'll go back and get her opinion on the snoo 🫡

You can just do non-conventional stuff in your home to stack movements throughout your day… I think of them as “free movements” bc I don’t have to think abt them or be intentional. I also sometimes think of them as setting little traps for myself hehe

New one - taking off the toilet paper holder and putting the roll behind you, on top of the toilet. 
Now going to the bathroom requires two spinal twists every time, one to get the roll and another one to put it back. It’s so simple and keeps your back twisty w/ little effort :)
People tend to be obsessed with making things easier and more convenient without much thought of the implications of that. Use it or lose it friends

A simple reframe you can do whenever something is not as convenient as it could be and you start to think “ugh this is annoying I have to change it” is to think “I get to do this extra movement! The world is offering me a chance to move in this sedentary time. I shall seize it😎”
It’s not “I have to do this”, it’s “I get to do this”. Move past trying to eliminate friction from your life and try to enjoy everything it brings you (in this case, mobility and patience) 🫶

One of my saddest school memories was learning the times tables

We had to write them all down from 1 to 10 and then recite them out loud, on a daily basis but (duh) I found it super boring and understimulating so after a few days of this

I started trying to have fun with it - at least thew writing portion

I would calculate the space I would need in the page for the 10 of them and where I would have to place the first digit of each column. then I would write all of the 1’s, 2’s and so on… all the ✖️’s… etc

until I had all of the basic expressions down: 5x2=__ 5x3=__ and so on
then I tried to have fun with the answers. My mom had shown me how the 9 times table always went one decimal up and one unit down, and I wanted to figure out if the others had cool patterns like that which

they did!! I even started to make grids and drawings to explore the patterns in the numbers and it was so fun!
eventually I knew the patterns, and anytime we had to write it down I would list all the expressions and then smugly make my way down filling out the answer column 😎

one day my teacher noticed what I was doing and got angry at me. she told me I couldn’t do it my way because I would never memorize the phrases. she said if she ever saw me do it that way again, I would have to start all over, painstakingly and tediously writing each expression💀

so I naturally started to hate times tables and by extension math with the force of a thousand suns! 🤷‍♀️ 
all the creativity and mystery and patterns were forbidden, and all that was left was a sad lifeless masochistic practice that tired my body and my mind

this was second grade and after that I only briefly liked some things in math class (largely depended on the teacher)
I imagine lots of you will have similar experiences, but I hope not.

It makes me so happy to see how vivid and joyful math is for kids in Montessori schools, how they’re encouraged to follow their curiosity and discover mathematical concepts through their own exploration, with a classroom full of beautiful enticing materials ❤️😭🥳
sidenote: what if montessori schools offered fun little math programs for adults?? I can think of nothing better to heal the school-math victims 🤔

all of the "parenting practices that are cool if you're rich and neglectful if you're poor" takes are curious AND also don't reflect anything about the value of said practices

there's stuff you do/don't do when you have less resources that is incidentally better for kids (like putting a mattress on the floor and not entertaining kids 24/7) and wealthier/ more educated people adopting it isn't some sort of "gotcha" moment, it's good societal progress

an example - to a child it makes no difference whether they have less toys because their parents can't afford more, or because they are intentionally restricting how much they purchase. the result is the same: the child is less overwhelmed and more creative in their play

a note here: even in Montessori schools, there are often large sets of open-ended materials, and this can seem harder to navigate bc unlike with the individual work trays it’s not clear that the child using eg some blocks has a say over the whole collection. however https://t.co/HiMT3UpDQ9

there are still much better ways to mediate this than “sharing is caring”
you can gently bring a child’s attention to the shared resources and encourage communication. you can help them make reasonable choices where both still feel respected :)

it can be as simple as “look, your friend is building a tower with these blocks. can you ask them if they want to work together with you?”
sometimes just giving them these tools avoids conflict altogether, without shaming anyone

I’m reading Hunt Gather Parent. so far it seems good! It put a smile on my face to know that the time-tested Mayan parenting strategies were all basically what we do in Montessori with toddlers and advise parents with.

some examples:
- let children see and participate in tasks even when it’s less convenient and they won’t do a great job
- treat them as a member of the family and include them in adult life
- give them real work to do, not a fake substitute
- don’t feel pressured to entertain

- state what you need them to do clearly, calmly, consistently, and give them time to respond
- ask for their help from a very young age (fetch things, carry groceries, etc.)
- give them opportunities to learn and remind yourself they want to help but don’t yet know how to

- let them observe you: show how things are done, then let them have a turn; when you show it focus on actions, explain less
- observe them closely: to know their skill level and adjust your mentoring
- intervene less, step away whenever you can

- encourage and ask the child to help, but never force it
- help the child if they ask for it, even if you know they could do it by themselves
- turn daily practical tasks into opportunities for connection and teamwork

I wonder if breastfeeding was easier in primitive communities where many nursing mothers could nurse your baby... like I wonder if the baby having varied feedback from more experienced moms would help them establish better latching and nursing 🤔

by feedback I mean the way moms adjust a baby's latch, try different positions etc. it seems to take quite a bit of time, frustration and pain for lots of new mothers and I'm curious whether that is a common human experience or if we had social tech for that
these are the sorts of questions I have for anthropologists who study moms and kids, but most don't seem very interested in the infant stage or at least not with the granularity I'd wish for

cross breastfeeding just seems smart... you're introducing variance in nutritional and immunological properties of the milk. I can't find anything on it historically (besides wet nurses which is diff bc it's still a single caretaker) or if it's practiced nowadays

one option would be to package all I've learned from my parent-baby course + observing at legit places that run these + running my own program, and then sell that relatively cheap online to 0-3 guides who already have the Montessori training down (current courses run for ~400$)

Since we’re on the topic, I love children and don’t like pretend play. It’s so boring 🤷‍♀️ I’ll play w/ a child for a bit if they ask me to, but then back off. I also distinctly remember as a child how pretend play was a magical space that could only be inhabited by other children.
Kids need adults for guidance, care and nurturing, and they need OTHER KIDS of diff ages to play with, as has always been the case with humans up until now. Parents =/= play peers

It’s fine to make the argument for parents as playmates in specific scenarios (ie single children in very isolated places) but if you try to convince me it’s the natural thing to do I’ll throw an anthropology book at you 😆

obviously play with your child when you feel like it and derive joy from it!! just don’t do it out of obligation and guilt. kids can play well alone or with others and in fact benefit a lot from that time.

@ShawnaaMariee_ oh yeah - as a nanny I would try to find distractions and look busy so I wasn’t overbearing on a child. but it was often confusing for the parents (and other adults nearby) who would assume my job was to constantly entertain the kid 😝

@majavonwestphal @_samantha_joy @edudissenter Montessori never wanted to separate “normal” and disabled children, that is simply the way it was at the time. Disabled children were kept in mental institutions alongside adults afaik, and she created a space specifically for them where it did not exist.

@majavonwestphal @_samantha_joy @edudissenter These children thrived. They benefitted immensely from her work, and she observed that many of the traits that others considered to be hopeless consequences of their mental conditions were actually a result of inadequate environments!

@majavonwestphal @_samantha_joy @edudissenter She then realized this was true for all children, not just disabled ones. Her environments were designed to empower every child, whether they were disabled or not, because they could get from the environment any mental nourishment they needed as individuals

@edudissenter @majavonwestphal @_samantha_joy What else can we go by in regard to that time? We have her testimony of the original schools, of course, and we have our experience with Montessori in practice currently, which seems to support her observations and thesis.

@majavonwestphal @_samantha_joy @edudissenter I think the language used, esp in her earlier stuff, can create this sense that she wanted to perfect and “fix” these disabled children… imo it’s mostly a language thing, between the time passed and translations. some of her original terms take on diff meanings nowadays.

I like the concept of exterogestation or a fourth trimester. I especially like that Maria Montessori kinda extended that idea into the first three years - what she called the “spiritual embryo”

she said that a child is born with a formed body and physical organs, but needs information about the environment that they’re born into in order to develop the organs of the mind, so they can become a person of “their place and time”

in a way this is like saying that children are born with universal ‘hardware’ and then during childhood they must develop custom ‘software’ that’s perfect for the needs of the context they’re in. maybe that’s obvious, but I still think it’s a cool way to frame it

in montessori education this comes up so much. parents are worried that children won't be "allowed to be children" because they are given the opportunity to (and often prefer to! *gasp*) do dishes, sweep floors, hang laundry, prepare food... instead of playing with toys https://t.co/WgWXlO7X69

I've also seen this be extended to academics. In a Montessori 3-6 classroom there are materials for academic-coded learning, and the kids are excited to use them and eager to start writing, reading, doing math operations... sometimes this upsets adults that think it's "too soon"

I'm not saying I think that very young girls buying into the whole consumerist, influencer-led image-based lifestyle is good. I think it's honestly a bit detrimental to them. But I also see a child successfully learning the ways of and adapting to her culture.

The same issue comes up with phones and screen addiction. Should we really criticize parents and children for foregoing precious childhood experiences like playing with toys in lieu of watching tiktoks, or do we have a bigger issue to address wrt to the values of our culture?

I have so many friends (~25-35) that *know* they want kids, are in solid long term relationships, but refrain from it due to fear and guilt. The reasons are almost always the same and it boils down to "I think my life needs to be perfect before I bring a child into it" 💔
in almost all cases it seems like an unreachable vision, an act of self-sabotage. you're never going to do something "once the conditions are right" if the conditions you set are a mirage.

among my friends' reasons I've heard:
- concern about the state of the world, climate change etc
- career and financial concerns (usually in a vague way)
- not wanting to pass down generational trauma
- philosophical troubles around the idea of bringing a child into existence

I'm glad my friends want to make responsible choices but I also wonder where this overpowering fear and cautiousness is coming from. Infohazards? Were there just one-too-many anti unplanned pregnancy campaigns in our youth? 🤔🤔

the biggest montessori pill is seeing under 2s pour themselves water from a glass pitcher to a glass cup, calmly get a towel to wipe up some that spilled, take the wet towel to a pail, come back to drink the water then carefully wash and dry their glass cup - all unprompted

and sometimes ppl will read this stuff and think montessori guides (or parents) expect toddlers to be at this level of performance all the time. ofc we don't!! they're baby humans! but the fact that they *can* do it and we rarely see it happen says a lot abt how we raise kids

the first time I saw a sequence like this happen in front of my eyes, in a montessori school, I silently cried tears of joy in my little observation chair... all the things I couldn't believe were true from the books and the training, right there before me 🥹

and it's not like children are repressed and doing all this stuff to please adults. gUYS they're doing this happily, peacefully, in deep concentration, to please themselves! they are so excited to do stuff without help and get better at it every day!! they're so cool!

a lot of things we believe about children in our society are just wrong, and a product of our environment. it's just as if we were to classify an animal's behavior as erratic, violent, lazy or chaotic after we put it in a habitat that is completely misadjusted to their needs.
(I think a lot is also misadjusted to adult needs, but we have experience and brain maturity on our side to help us deal with stuff better)

If you know you're going to need a stroller and are deciding on which one to purchase, there's something you might not have considered yet (and that most retailers won't mention): the *shape* of the seat and of its recline!
Let me explain why this matters ⬇️ https://t.co/zZ8lAQUsmt

Seats like 1 or 2 allow you to change the relationship between the backboard and baseboard. this means your baby's hips get to be at different angles depending on the recline - and variation is KEY in an already sedentary mode of transport! https://t.co/CtyVosmteZ

This *also* means your kid can prop themselves up from time to time and sit forward using their core and back, or lean forward holding onto a bar (not pictured) which provides even more variation to the shape of the body and its loads. 💪 https://t.co/66dAyp68hw

In contrast, seats like 3 ("bucket seats") have a solid outer frame around it, and recline in such a way that keeps baby's hips in a fixed position whether they are sitting or lying down for a nap. Because of this, they also make it harder for them to lean forward or sit up :( https://t.co/OzTbkAxWup

Is this always a problem? I'd say if your stroller use is very light and your kid spends most of their day moving and unconstrained by other types of bucket seats (carseats, rockers, loungers, etc.) then it's probably ok to go for something like 3... however,

If you tend to rely on a stroller a lot, or your kid already spends a lot of time in a bucket seat as is, try to choose one like 1 or 2 that gives them as much optionality and freedom of movement as possible, so they can still use their body a bit while being pushed around :)

I was talking to my mom abt my frustrations with my driving instructor and suddenly remembered how at one point my parents had to explicitly tell me that the school was mostly teaching me things in order to pass tests and not because they wanted me to learn things :--)

I remember now, little B's frustration coming home from school when the teachers made me do exercises I had already mastered, write in answers that I felt were wrong and didn't let me argue about it, didn't let me use alternative explanations... it's all the same, ofc

it's useless to feel frustrated at or question the driving instructor because his role is just to make me pass the test. maybe this sounds ridiculously obvious to you, but somehow this is never my default. I always assume the point is to pursue the truth like a LOSER and a NERD

some controversial baby-rearing practices that I think are pretty cool and based:

- montessori floor bed
- elimination communication
- baby-led weaning
- glass and ceramic tableware
- no socks or mittens
- minimal babyproofing

(my reasons why below 🧵) https://t.co/Vb6gdSsTBz

babies don't need cribs! a montessori floor bed is really just a thin mattress on the floor, and it has multiple benefits:
- freedom of movement
- autonomy
- spatial awareness
- easier for parent to lay down and cuddle baby
- safer (no bars or height)
- lower cost https://t.co/PlXUZuP7WA
(also, yes - baby will fall sleep on the floor sometimes... it's ok!) https://t.co/VkGUpN7wLk

elimination communication, or 'natural hygiene' is a practice based on learning and following your baby's cues wrt eliminating (peeing and pooping). it's often described as infant potty training, but in reality it's more like avoiding conditioning a baby to use a diaper https://t.co/JMnstzfpvB

this is also a really cool practice to try out if you can! some of the benefits are:
- increased (or... maintained?) bodily awareness for the baby
- increased awareness of the baby's communication for the parent
- less (especially poopy) diapers to throw out or wash

baby-led weaning is basically just feeding your baby regular food (or a slightly modified) instead of special foods and purees! it helps:
- encourage the correct swallowing pattern
- eye hand coordination
- jaw development
- prevent picky eating
plus it's easier to prepare! https://t.co/ziwtPOUDaE

it's also great for helping babies become joyful, confident, autonomous eaters who are aware of their own preferences and appetites
solid starts is a great blw resource: https://t.co/MtrO77f9ec

on the topic, in montessori we use REAL tableware with babies from the beginning: glass cups, ceramic plates, stainless steel utensils...
babies can use these just fine, and they help understand cause and effect - because misuse will result in loud sounds or even breaking https://t.co/WZPNgFd3iJ
"While there is more risk with a young child holding a glass, there is also more opportunity to learn and develop respect, precision, and control of movement." read -> https://t.co/XJ5U1Sw3zk

why no socks or mittens tho? 
obv if it's freezing, put some socks on your baby! but generally speaking, it's best to avoid covering their hands or feet if the weather allows: babies' hands and feet have a LOT of nerve endings that are crucial for sensory and motor development

for newborns especially, the hands are a point of reference - some of the only things that are still constant from their "before life" in the womb. preserving their access to their hands (and filing nails frequently) helps them maintain this sense of familiarity and cosiness https://t.co/RHLRV1piIU

when babies start crawling, we often think of all of the dangers they might suddenly encounter. but it's good to take a step back and look at what are risks vs actual dangers. when a baby interacts with the environment and takes risks, they learn through natural consequences. https://t.co/NNYvXyxEfC

ofc it's great to have some sections of the house be totally baby-safe ("Yes spaces"!), but babies also need to learn how to deal with obstacles. for example, they can learn to go up and down stairs safely if we give them time and space to practice: https://t.co/hcLkM2gK1w

when babies are trusted to practice doing hard things from early on - be it sleeping without bars around them, eating solid pieces of food, handling breakable items or navigating obstacles - it can surprise us to learn just how capable they are!

@mbateman ofc, this happens sometimes. it can *usually* be fixed by making the room less exciting and more sleep conducive (removing toys etc) and having a parent guide the routine gradually. but as always every baby is a person with individual needs that we need to follow above all :)

@mbateman I love it so much!! I've personally seen so many babies that used to wake up from naps crying in their cribs for someone to pick them up transition to peacefully playing until someone notices they're awake... just seems like the logical, respectful thing to do

@PhillipBrandon all your life? :p
in general, avoiding socks and shoes up until baby has been walking for 6 solid months is best. but if you can't fsr, going for thin-soled shoes (like leather mocassins or similar) during that first period is second-best. socks should at least have grippy soles
@PhillipBrandon there are socks with grippy parts on the tops of the toes that work for crawling babies too, so it prevents them from slipping around which can rlly get in the way of moving well
they are also way more GRIPPY with bare hands and feet! grippiness is crucial when you're a baby who's still developing balance and motor control https://t.co/4DK83UhCxI
@macck_attack they really can, as long as the mattress is firm enough and there are no gaps or soft bedding around 🥳
@macck_attack once they start rolling over you can lay a foam mat or a rug next to the bed, but pretty soon they also learn how to navigate the limits of the mattress https://t.co/ll49bGrlFW

@AskYatharth I had the same experience as you, and quickly realized the level of power I have even in these subtle moves, and the care I must wield it with. there is a delicate balance between discarding and abusing power... but you have to find it in order to lead and be followed with grace
@AskYatharth the other thing about not phrasing directions/limits as requests is that it makes actual requests and choice much more powerful and truthful. kids are extremely sensitive to this

@AskYatharth ever since I learned this, I'm also more in tune to how people in general need this. I find myself giving this advice in all scenarios: people react well to decisiveness and firm boundaries, if they feel wobbliness they keep prodding and grasping for something solid

@AskYatharth if we clearly state the direction/limit early we disclose the rules of the game and allow the other person to earnestly accept it or contest it. but if we tiptoe around it, we hide the limit and make room for lots of frustration as the other person tries to figure it out

maybe this is basic to everyone else but a lesson children have taught me and that keeps showing up in every part of my life is that we need to communicate clearly and own the limits we set. (whether the other person accepts them or not is a diff story) https://t.co/6d8Be9OcLt

I'm a Montessori 0-3 years guide and *slightly* obsessed with materials. Here are some for babies and toddlers that can actually make sense to have at home, and a bit about how we can design and choose objects for kids based on their development! 🧵 https://t.co/1NPN4cJX5P

The topponcino is a thin oval-shaped mattress or pillow that can be used to hold baby in the first few weeks, preserving a familiar scent, temperature and feeling - baby’s points of reference, that help her feel safe - and that also works wonders for setting babies down. https://t.co/JnPGgdZ3hF

There is a scope and sequence of visual, then tactile mobiles for babies in the Montessori “repertoire”. This is the dancers mobile, an all-time favorite. Baby practices visual tracking as he follows the moving shapes with his eyes, and focusing on objects at different distances. https://t.co/uOHiaQ6enm

The bell is the first tactile mobile that we present - as baby starts batting at it with her hands, she gradually makes the connection between her movements and the sound, learning about cause and effect - which is rewarding and intriguing!! https://t.co/jxcLzDf2jd
The ring is a bit more advanced, requiring the baby to open her hand and close the fingers around the ring in order to move it around, now that she is able to bring her hands to her midline.

After baby is able to turn her head and coordinate her arms and hands to grab objects from the ground, we start to offer rattles in which the actual bells or parts making sound are visible! That way she can see how the object responds to her movement and produces sound. https://t.co/tKywNRImzv

As the baby starts to manipulate objects with both hands and bringing them to her mouth, we offer a grasping ball with protrusions which make it easier to grab, and help it not roll too far away and land just within reach from baby before she is confidently crawling after it. https://t.co/UoCRFd0ADx

A placemat like this means toddlers are able to directly see where things go, and check it against the traced shapes - providing the child with control of error. This means more opportunities for independence, and less direct coaching or correction by adults! https://t.co/Ocs6eZ3YOh

The point of the ball tracker is not only to develop visual tracking skills and hand-eye coordination... toddlers have to stand in order to drop the ball in, and then squat to get it back from the bottom. All this coordinated, effortful movement is pure brain+body nutrition! https://t.co/j6mDjFQSyf

Notice how the stem in the center of this stacker is cone-shaped? This means that the rings, of different circumferences, will only fit at a certain part of the stacker and in a particular order. Control of error, baby! https://t.co/hbACuS8tbe

Most shape sorters try to pack too much into a single object - colors, patterns, letters, numbers... In Montessori, we prefer materials that isolate difficulty - which means they allow a child to focus on the precise thing they’re trying to do (in this case, shape discrimination) https://t.co/ZcdgiATGoR

The first puzzles we offer toddlers are these framed, single-shape ones- which also have a scope and sequence. We introduce the circle first bc it can fit in any orientation. The square requires some wrist rotation to align with its slot, and the triangle requires the most! https://t.co/rTeGKVXaLP

Once these are mastered, we offer puzzles with several geometric shapes (where the toddler works on figuring out which shape goes where), the same shape in different sizes (size discrimination) and later start introducing more complex ones like animals, that will only fit one way
Here is a more in-depth article where you can learn more about each of these materials and what I think about buying Montessori materials for using at home: https://t.co/Vygzajq1JP

thinking out loud - I feel like I want to focus my energy on creating parenting-sustaining community/services and parenting culture in my local area, unclear what the best way to proceed is

what I mean by this is any type of structure, event, space or scene that helps parents... and in turn helps to make parenting seem attainable to young people who are scared of having kids and losing their social life / mental health / etc

I think this is bigger than straight up financial constraints for most people. A support network can make parenting much more affordable - used baby gear, toys and clothes, free neighbor-babysitting or shared nannies, better mental health from being less alone!

Also there is an effect where people start to have kids once their friends, siblings, cousins and neighbors have kids... we underestimate the power of familiarity when it comes to big life changes. low-birthrate places are self-fulfilling prophecies

Having kids around, and having age-peer parents around makes it seem *possible* to be a parent, makes people feel less alone in taking the jump, it creates a culture that wasn't there before. I want to know if I can catalyse this and how

and before someone tells me to have kids myself - I'm working on physical health issues!! I wish I could start now but alas, I need to recover some more 😅 I figure I can still do pro-parent stuff in the meantime as a early childhood specialist and baby-forward person

I often get asked how to 'help' babies roll, crawl or sit... and my reply is always this: time and space on the floor, a firm surface, comfortable clothing, interesting objects, parent observing but not interfering. I'll explain why below
https://t.co/5oGgFI8COg

babies need time on the floor, where they are free to look around their surroundings and move without external restrictions. giving them this time, incrementally, from birth, helps get them acquainted with the floor, their body and the relationship between the two.

the surface on the floor should be firm enough that baby's movement attempts are successful: a too-cushy foam mat or too-soft blanket will cause more difficulty for a baby wanting to roll over or push off with their feet to crawl.

clothing is the other aspect that is often overlooked. it's important to check that the feet and knees have enough grip (uncovered ideally), that the clothing allows baby to twist and extend both arms and legs, and that there is not too much fabric inhibiting vision or movement.

having objects on the floor that the baby can explore - in different shapes, materials and colors - keeps them interested and gives them something to focus on. instead of wiggling toys around as 'bait', or handing them directly to baby, lay them out just close enough to reach.

the last step might be the most important: caregivers need to give babies space so they can figure things out themselves. *and* they can use this distance to observe: to understand what the baby is trying to do, and to understand what might be blocking them.
this is one of my favorite quotes about babies and movement, by dr. emmi pikler https://t.co/tJFsr89ArV

this learning process involves a great deal of time, persistence and experience. we can help by not placing babies in restrictive devices such as seats, carriers, etc for long periods of time, and in positions they aren't able to reach or sustain on their own.

it can sometimes be frustrating to watch a baby trying to roll over and getting their arm stuck over and over again, but this is precisely what they need to experience in order to master their body, develop self-confidence, and see movement as a source of agency and joy!

there are three things a baby needs in order to move freely:

- a body that can move
- an environment (read: physical space *and* caregivers) that allows movement
- a desire to move

babies get bored and they get undermoved. we need to talk about this more

I have this pet theory that babies' vestibular systems are "expecting" to be carried around by an adult on foot basically the whole day and when they don't get that input they need to be bounced and rocked
also curious how in our culture when a baby cries we think "sleep, hunger, diaper,  gas" but we don't think "movement, boredom" - yet in other cultures it's different!
basically I think there is such a thing as too little physical and mental stimulation for a baby and it's a much more common occurrence than we'd like to think

maybe the real issue with ipad babies is our sedentary and repetitive way of life, actually, so kids (and adults) get addicted to screens because that's the most stimulating and exciting thing they've got going on

@mbateman this is true!! it's the "transport response" - babies' heart rate decreases whenever they sense that the adult carrying them is upright and walking, kinda like what happens when mother cats carry kittens by the nape of their necks

@OneEyedAlpaca I think a good way to frame this is: if a restaurant (like an all you can eat buffet type place) did this to you, would you feel like they trusted you?

@OneEyedAlpaca I personally don't think it sends the right message, can lead to some disordered eating as ppl have pointed out (I was never fined but reprimanded for it and now struggle to leave things on my plate after I'm full) and would be better as a frank ongoing conversation as they grow

@OneEyedAlpaca little kids are still calibrating how much to serve themselves because they're not super familiar with their body's signals. when they start to serve themselves I like to say 'just get a little bit and if you want more you can always get more!' and later talk about why

@OneEyedAlpaca maybe it's worth elaborating: in the case you suggested, they will most likely see the offense as "not eating everything I put on my plate" instead of "filling up my plate too much" or when they realize they estimated incorrectly the only way to not be fined will be to overeat
